plants.note:
Genus-level entry; multiple species used interchangeably under the name
bailahuén in Chile

Matricaria recutita and Matricaria chamomilla are treated as synonyms; this
entry covers both names as used in Chilean DS N°3/10

Chilean DS N°3/10 lists both Ribes cucullatum and Ribes punctatum under
the name zarzaparrilla

Chilean DS N°3/10 lists both Rumex conglomeratus and Rumex crispus
under romaza

Chilean DS N°3/10 lists Ruta chalepensis specifically. Cross-regional ruda
entries in previous JSON reference Ruta graveolens; these are closely related but
distinct species — see ambiguity flag

Chilean DS N°3/10 lists both Salix humboldtiana and Salix chilensis under
sauce amargo

Chilean DS N°3/10 lists Schinus areira under molle. Cross-regional entry
previously used Schinus molle. These are closely related but taxonomically distinct; see
ambiguity flag

Chilean DS N°3/10 includes S. crispum and S. gayanum as synonyms
under natre

names.name: Anís
names.ambiguity_note:
Anís in Chile refers to Pimpinella anisum but
the name is sometimes applied to Foeniculum vulgare (hinojo) by lay users

names.name: Arrayán
names.ambiguity_note:
Arrayán in Chile is primarily
Luma chequen (chequén) but the name is applied to other Myrtaceae species
regionally

names.name: Artemisa
names.ambiguity_note:
Artemisa in Chile refers to Tanacetum
parthenium per DS N°3/10, but the same name is widely used across South America for
Artemisia spp., creating high ambiguity risk

names.name: Bailahuén
names.ambiguity_note:
Multiple Haplopappus species
used interchangeably; H. baylahuen most commonly cited but not always distinguished
from congeners in market trade

names.name: Canelo
names.ambiguity_note:
Canelo in Chile refers to
Drimys winteri (sacred Mapuche tree) but the same word is used globally for
Cinnamomum verum (cinnamon tree); context is required to disambiguate

names.name: Chépica
names.ambiguity_note:
Chépica in Chile is Paspalum
vaginatum for medicinal use, but the common name is also applied generically to lawn
grasses; non-specialist use may refer to other grass species

names.name: Contrayerba
names.ambiguity_note:
Contrayerba is a highly ambiguous
name used across Latin America for multiple unrelated species. In Chile's DS N°3/10 it
maps to Flaveria bidentis, but elsewhere it commonly refers to Dorstenia contrayerva
and other species

names.name: Guayacán
names.ambiguity_note:
Guayacán in Chile
refers to Porlieria chilensis, but the name is used in other countries for unrelated
species including Guaiacum officinale and Bulnesia sarmientoi

names.name: Hierba dulce
names.ambiguity_note:
Hierba dulce
in Chile refers to Calceolaria thyrsiflora (endemic), but the same name is used in other
countries for Stevia rebaudiana and other sweet-tasting plants

names.name: Lampazo
names.ambiguity_note:
Lampazo in Chile is used for both
Senecio fistulosus (hualtata/lampazo) and Arctium lappa (bardana/lampazo); two
entirely different species share this name

names.name: Llantén
names.ambiguity_note:
Llantén without qualifier
in Chile most often refers to Plantago major; see llantén menor and siete venas for
Plantago lanceolata

names.name: Llareta
names.ambiguity_note:
Llareta refers to Laretia
acaulis in Chile; in Bolivia and Peru the same name refers to Azorella compacta, a
different cushion plant of the same family

names.name: Yareta
names.ambiguity_note:
Shared name with Azorella compacta in Bolivia and
Peru; see llareta ambiguity note

names.name: Manzanilla
names.ambiguity_note:
Chilean DS N°3/10 lists Matricaria
recutita. Market products may contain Chamaemelum nobile (Roman chamomile)
without distinction

names.name: Mastuerzo
names.ambiguity_note:
Mastuerzo in Chile (DS N°3/10) refers to Capsella
bursa-pastoris. In other Spanish-speaking countries mastuerzo is commonly Lepidium
sativum or Tropaeolum majus

names.name: Matico
names.ambiguity_note:
In Chile matico
= Buddleja globosa (pañil). In Peru matico = Piper aduncum. Same name, different 
species, different families

names.name: Menta
names.ambiguity_note:
Menta in Chile per DS
N°3/10 refers to Mentha x piperita. Generic menta without qualifier may also refer to
Mentha spicata in some contexts

names.name: Molle
names.ambiguity_note:
Molle in Chile (DS N°3/10) is
Schinus areira. In Peru and Bolivia molle is Schinus molle. In Argentina it is used for
several Schinus species. Cross-regional database entries must track country

names.name: Paico
names.ambiguity_note:
Chilean DS N°3/10 lists Chenopodium
chilense as paico, a Chilean-specific species. The same name in Peru, Argentina, and
Bolivia refers to Dysphania ambrosioides, a different though related species. Do not
merge these without country qualifier

names.name: Pino abeto
names.ambiguity_note:
Pinus radiata is not a true
abeto (Abies spp.); this Chilean common name is a misnomer. The name pino or pino
abeto may be applied loosely to other introduced conifers

names.name: Quebracho blanco
names.ambiguity_note:
Quebracho blanco in Chile
(DS N°3/10) refers to Senna stipulacea. In Argentina quebracho blanco is
Aspidosperma quebracho-blanco, a completely different tree. High ambiguity across
countries

names.name: Retamo
names.ambiguity_note:
Retamo is used for Spartium junceum in 
Chile, but in other South American countries retamo may refer to Teline monspessulana
or Cytisus scoparius; all are highly toxic broom-family plants

names.name: Ruda
names.ambiguity_note:
Chilean DS N°3/10 specifies Ruta
chalepensis. Previous cross-regional entries reference Ruta graveolens. Both species
share the name ruda across South America and have similar pharmacological profiles,
but are distinct; country-tagged entries must use the correct canonical

names.name: Salvia
names.ambiguity_note:
Salvia in Chile (DS N°3/10)
is Salvia officinalis. Across Latin America salvia is applied loosely to many Salvia
species including native species such as Salvia rosmarinus and S. hispanica

names.name: Alejandría
names.ambiguity_note:
Alejandría in Chile specifically refers to the
fruit-with-seed preparation of Senna alexandrina, distinct from sen (leaves)

names.name: Toronjil
names.ambiguity_note:
In Chile, toronjil per DS
N°3/10 is Melissa officinalis (entry 94). However the document also lists Marrubium
vulgare as toronjil cuyano (entry 93), creating an intra-Chilean naming split. Crossregional toronjil ambiguity persists as noted in prior rounds

names.name: Toronjil cuyano
names.ambiguity_note:
Toronjil
cuyano specifically disambiguates Marrubium vulgare from Melissa officinalis (toronjil) in
Chile; without the cuyano qualifier, toronjil defaults to melisa

names.name: Tusílago
names.ambiguity_note:
Tusílago in Chile (DS 
N°3/10) refers to Petasites fragrans. Classically and internationally tusílago = Tussilago
farfara, a different species. Chilean usage is non-standard and may cause crossregional confusion

names.name: Valeriana
names.ambiguity_note:
Same ambiguity as noted in
prior rounds; native Valeriana species in Andean Chile may be sold under the same
name

names.name: Verbena
names.ambiguity_note:
Chilean DS N°3/10
lists Verbena litoralis. Verbena officinalis (European verbena) shares the common name
and is imported. Aloysia citrodora (cedrón) is occasionally called verbena olorosa in
Chile, adding a third dimension to this name cluster

names.name: Calaguala
names.ambiguity_note:
Calaguala in Chile is Polypodium feuillei.
In Peru and Colombia calaguala refers to Phlebodium pseudoaureum or related ferns.
Same common name, different species across the Andes

names.name: Zarzaparrilla
names.ambiguity_note:
Zarzaparrilla is one of the most
ambiguous names in Latin American herbalism. In Chile (DS N°3/10) it refers to Ribes
cucullatum and R. punctatum. In other countries it refers to Smilax spp. (the classical
sarsaparilla). These are entirely different genera and families
